0.body: This guide is a resource for people living with Long COVID, their caregivers, and anyone who wants to learn more about the condition. Long COVID (also called Post-COVID Conditions or PASC) affects millions of people worldwide. This site aims to gather practical information, evidence-based resources, and community support in one place.
0.title: Introduction

1.body: Use the sidebar to navigate between sections: Understanding Long COVID — what it is, common symptoms, and who is affected Management & Treatment — practical strategies and medical options Resources — research links, support groups, and useful tools
1.breadcrumbs: Introduction » How to Use This Guide
1.title: How to Use This Guide

10.body: Shortness of breath or breathlessness Persistent cough
10.title: Respiratory

11.body: Extreme fatigue not relieved by rest Post-exertional malaise (PEM) — worsening of symptoms after physical or mental activity
11.title: Energy & Fatigue

12.body: Joint or muscle pain Gastrointestinal issues Changes in taste or smell Anxiety and depression Note: Post-exertional malaise (PEM) is a hallmark symptom that sets Long COVID apart from typical fatigue. Pushing through PEM can worsen the condition significantly.
12.title: Other Common Symptoms

13.body: Long COVID can affect anyone who has had COVID-19, regardless of age, prior health status, or severity of the acute illness.
13.breadcrumbs: Who is Affected » Who is Affected
13.title: Who is Affected

14.body: Estimates suggest 10–30% of people who contract COVID-19 develop some form of Long COVID Millions of people globally are affected People who were never hospitalized make up the majority of Long COVID cases
14.title: Prevalence

15.body: Research has identified several factors associated with higher risk: Female sex Older age Having had a more severe acute illness Pre-existing health conditions (e.g., asthma, diabetes) Not being vaccinated against COVID-19 Vaccination has been shown to reduce (though not eliminate) the risk of developing Long COVID.
15.title: Risk Factors

16.body: Long COVID can range from mild inconvenience to complete disability. Many people: Are unable to work or have reduced work capacity Experience significant disruption to social and family life Face challenges getting an accurate diagnosis and appropriate care
16.breadcrumbs: Who is Affected » Impact on Daily Life
16.title: Impact on Daily Life

17.body: Pacing is one of the most important self-management strategies for Long COVID, especially for those experiencing post-exertional malaise (PEM).
17.breadcrumbs: Pacing and Energy Management » Pacing and Energy Management
17.title: Pacing and Energy Management

18.body: Pacing means staying within your available energy limits to avoid triggering PEM. The goal is to find a sustainable activity level that doesn't cause crashes.
18.breadcrumbs: Pacing and Energy Management » What is Pacing?
18.title: What is Pacing?

19.body: Think of your daily energy as a fixed "envelope." Activities — physical, cognitive, and emotional — all draw from this envelope. Pacing means: Staying within the envelope, not pushing to its edges Resting before you feel exhausted, not after Spreading activities throughout the day
19.breadcrumbs: Pacing and Energy Management » The Energy Envelope
19.title: The Energy Envelope

2.body: This is an open resource. If you'd like to contribute or suggest improvements, please visit the GitHub repository .
2.title: Contributing

20.body: Many people use heart rate (HR) as a pacing guide. Staying below your anaerobic threshold can help prevent PEM. A commonly used estimate: Maximum HR for pacing = (220 - age) × 0.6 Wearables (Garmin, Apple Watch, Fitbit) can help track this in real time.
20.breadcrumbs: Pacing and Energy Management » Heart Rate Monitoring
20.title: Heart Rate Monitoring

21.body: Break tasks into smaller chunks with rest breaks between them Prioritize — identify what truly needs to be done vs. what can wait Cognitive rest counts too — reduce screen time, noise, and multitasking Keep an activity/symptom diary to find your personal patterns Avoid the boom-bust cycle — good days are not a sign to overdo it
21.breadcrumbs: Pacing and Energy Management » Practical Tips
21.title: Practical Tips

22.body: A useful framework from occupational therapy: Prioritize what matters most Plan activities in advance to avoid last-minute rushing Pace yourself throughout each activity
22.breadcrumbs: Pacing and Energy Management » The Three P's
22.title: The Three P's

23.body: There is currently no universally approved treatment for Long COVID. Management is largely symptom-focused. Work with your healthcare provider to build a plan suited to your specific symptoms.
23.title: Medical Treatments

24.body: Symptom Area Approaches Being Studied Fatigue / PEM Low-dose naltrexone (LDN), pacing protocols Dysautonomia / POTS Beta-blockers, increased salt/fluid intake, compression garments Brain fog Cognitive rehabilitation, antihistamines Sleep Sleep hygiene, melatonin, CBT-I Pain Anti-inflammatories, physical therapy Depression/Anxiety SSRIs, therapy Important: Graded Exercise Therapy (GET) has been shown to worsen outcomes in people with PEM. Discuss any exercise plan carefully with your doctor.
24.title: Currently Investigated Treatments

25.body: You may benefit from referrals to: Cardiologist (for POTS, chest symptoms) Neurologist (for brain fog, neuropathy, headaches) Pulmonologist (for breathing symptoms) Rheumatologist (for immune/inflammatory issues) Psychiatrist or psychologist (for mental health support)
25.title: Specialist Referrals

26.body: Active trials are listed at ClinicalTrials.gov — search "Long COVID" or "PASC" for currently enrolling studies.
26.title: Clinical Trials

27.body: Keep records to bring to appointments: A symptom diary with dates, severity, and triggers List of all medications and supplements Records of previous test results
27.title: Documenting Your Symptoms

28.body: Living with a chronic, often misunderstood illness takes a significant mental and emotional toll. Addressing mental health is an important part of Long COVID management.
28.title: Mental Health

29.body: Depression — from illness burden, loss of function, and uncertainty Anxiety — about symptoms, recovery, and the future Grief — for your pre-illness self and lost activities Medical gaslighting — being dismissed or disbelieved by healthcare providers Isolation — from reduced capacity to socialize
29.breadcrumbs: Mental Health » Common Mental Health Challenges
29.title: Common Mental Health Challenges

3.body: Long COVID refers to a wide range of health problems that persist for four or more weeks after an acute COVID-19 infection. It can affect people regardless of the severity of their initial illness — including those who had mild or asymptomatic cases.
3.breadcrumbs: What is Long COVID? » What is Long COVID?
3.title: What is Long COVID?

30.body: Talk to your GP or primary care provider about mental health symptoms Ask for a referral to a therapist or psychologist experienced with chronic illness Peer support groups (online or local) can provide understanding and practical advice
30.title: Seeking Support

31.body: Validate your experience — Long COVID is real, even when tests are normal Set boundaries — it's okay to decline activities that will cause a crash Connect with others — Long COVID communities online can reduce isolation Celebrate small wins — recovery is often non-linear
31.title: Self-Care Strategies

32.body: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) can help with coping strategies for chronic illness. However, CBT approaches that challenge illness beliefs or push activity levels may not be appropriate for Long COVID. Look for therapists familiar with chronic fatigue conditions.
32.breadcrumbs: Mental Health » Note on CBT and Therapy
32.title: Note on CBT and Therapy

33.body: A selection of key research and ongoing studies on Long COVID.
33.title: Research & Studies

34.body: RECOVER Initiative (NIH) — large US-based observational and clinical trial program for Long COVID UK Biobank Long COVID studies — population-level data on prevalence and risk factors Ziyad Al-Aly studies (VA/NEJM) — comprehensive analyses of Long COVID outcomes
34.title: Landmark Studies

35.body: Long COVID affects multiple organ systems and can occur after mild infection Vaccination reduces but does not eliminate Long COVID risk Distinct biological abnormalities have been found in Long COVID patients (microclots, T-cell dysregulation, viral persistence) ME/CFS and Long COVID share significant biological overlap
35.title: Key Findings

36.body: medRxiv — preprints (not yet peer-reviewed) The Lancet Nature Medicine NEJM
36.title: Preprint Servers & Journals

37.body: Search ClinicalTrials.gov for actively enrolling Long COVID studies.
37.title: Ongoing Trials

38.body: You are not alone. There are many communities of Long COVID patients, advocates, and researchers working together.
38.title: Support Groups

39.body: Long COVID Support — large Facebook group for patients and caregivers Body Politic — patient-led COVID-19 support group and research collective Long COVID Alliance — coalition of patient and research organizations ME/CFS & Long COVID subreddit — r/covidlonghaulers on Reddit
39.title: Online Communities

4.body: Symptoms last weeks, months, or longer after the initial infection Can affect multiple organ systems simultaneously Symptoms may relapse and remit or fluctuate over time Not explained by an alternative diagnosis
4.breadcrumbs: What is Long COVID? » Key Characteristics
4.title: Key Characteristics

40.body: Patient-Led Research Collaborative — patient-researchers studying Long COVID Long COVID Alliance — US-based advocacy and research coalition Long Covid SOS (UK) — advocacy group raising awareness
40.title: Patient Organizations

41.body: Ask your doctor if there is a local Long COVID clinic or support group Check with local hospitals — many have established dedicated Long COVID services Local ME/CFS groups often welcome Long COVID patients, given symptom overlap
41.title: Finding Local Support

42.body: A curated list of external resources.
42.title: Useful Links

43.body: CDC — Post-COVID Conditions WHO — Post COVID-19 condition NIH RECOVER Initiative
43.title: Official Health Organizations

44.title: Patient Resources

45.body: Visible App — pacing and heart rate monitoring for PEM Bearable — symptom and health tracking app ClinicalTrials.gov — find active trials
45.title: Tools & Apps

46.body: Long COVID: major findings, mechanisms and recommendations (Nature Reviews, 2023)
46.title: Further Reading

5.body: The WHO defines Post COVID-19 condition as occurring "usually 3 months from the onset of COVID-19, with symptoms that last for at least 2 months and cannot be explained by an alternative diagnosis." The CDC uses the term "Post-COVID Conditions" for the wide range of health consequences present four or more weeks after infection.
5.breadcrumbs: What is Long COVID? » Official Definitions
5.title: Official Definitions

6.body: Researchers are investigating several potential causes, including: Viral persistence or viral reservoirs Immune dysregulation and chronic inflammation Microbiome disruption Reactivation of latent viruses (e.g., EBV) Autoantibody formation Microclots and vascular damage
6.breadcrumbs: What is Long COVID? » Possible Mechanisms
6.title: Possible Mechanisms

7.body: Long COVID can present with over 200 reported symptoms. The most commonly reported include:
7.title: Common Symptoms

8.body: Brain fog (difficulty thinking, concentrating, or remembering) Headaches Sleep disturbances Dizziness or lightheadedness
8.title: Neurological & Cognitive

9.body: Heart palpitations or racing heart Postural Orthostatic Tachycardia Syndrome (POTS) Chest pain or tightness
9.title: Cardiovascular & Autonomic
